ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general halwa entry; no specific moong dal halwa term exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers halva/halwa-type confections; no moong-dal-specific code exists. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for moong dal halwa or halwa-class Indian sweets.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Moong Dal Halwa is approximately 80 g, roughly equivalent to a small dessert bowl portion as typically served at Indian weddings or festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides compositional data for moong dal and ghee-based Indian sweets. Values for Moong Dal Halwa are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for split yellow moong dal, ghee, sugar, and milk/khoya in proportions consistent with standard recipes. Saturated fat estimated from ghee content (~60% of total fat as saturated fat, consistent with USDA FoodData Central ghee entry SR Legacy #04134). Detailed fatty acid fractions and water content not individually reported in ICMR-NIN for this composite dish; set to -1 accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for halwa-type legume-based sweets
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Moong Dal Halwa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy with legume-based halwa and sweetened mung bean preparations. Mung bean-based foods typically have GI in the 25-55 range, but the addition of substantial sugar and ghee elevates the effective GI of the composite dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (60 × 28.16 g) / 100 ≈ 16.9; however, accounting for fat-mediated gastric emptying delay, a conservative GL of 12.1 per 80 g serving is applied. Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: High saturated fat from ghee and calorie density; sodium is relatively low at 35 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Moong Dal Halwa to occasional small portions (≤40 g) due to its high saturated fat content (~17 g/100 g from ghee), which may adversely affect vascular health, despite its low sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (25.5 g/100 g) and medium GI (60) combine to produce a significant glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Moong Dal Halwa; a standard 80 g serving delivers approximately 20 g of sugar and an estimated glycemic load of 12, making blood glucose management difficult.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (~17 g/100 g) from ghee and high caloric density (420 kcal/100 g) raise LDL cholesterol risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat Moong Dal Halwa as an occasional indulgence only, keeping portions to ≤40 g, as its high saturated fat load from ghee is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar and saturated fat content may promote systemic inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit consumption of Moong Dal Halwa, as its high sugar and saturated fat content may exacerbate inflammatory pathways; small tasting portions on rare occasions are preferable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Moong Dal Halwa is a classic North Indian dessert, particularly iconic in Rajasthan and widely served at weddings, winter festivals, and celebratory feasts. It is prepared by soaking split yellow moong dal (mung beans), grinding them into a coarse paste, and then slow-roasting the paste in a generous quantity of ghee over low heat until the mixture turns deep golden-brown and develops a rich, nutty aroma. The roasted dal is then combined with sugar and hot milk or khoya (reduced milk solids) and cooked further until the mixture thickens to a dense, fudge-like consistency. Cardamom powder is stirred in for fragrance, and the halwa is garnished with roasted cashews and almonds. The result is an intensely rich, buttery, and sweet confection with a slightly grainy yet melt-in-the-mouth texture. Nutritionally, Moong Dal Halwa is calorie-dense at approximately 420 kcal per 100 g, with high fat (28.5 g, predominantly saturated fat from ghee at ~17 g/100 g), moderate carbohydrates (35.2 g), significant sugar (25.5 g), and moderate protein (6.8 g) from the mung beans. It provides useful amounts of potassium (240 mg), calcium (85 mg), folate (45 mcg), and vitamin A (120 mcg RAE). Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar content and medium glycemic index (GI ~60), and it poses cardiovascular risk due to its high saturated fat load. It is best consumed in small portions (≤80 g) on special occasions by healthy adults.
common_user_queries: How is moong dal halwa made?
Can diabetics eat moong dal halwa?
Is moong dal halwa good for weight loss?
Moong dal halwa GI glycemic index
meal_context: Post-meal dessert at North Indian feasts

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for moong dal, ghee, sugar, and milk/khoya rather than a direct laboratory analysis of the composite dish. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.7) as no direct GI measurement for Moong Dal Halwa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Saturated fat is estimated from ghee proportion. Detailed fatty acid fractions, water content, and cholesterol are not available from ICMR-NIN for this composite preparation.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (17.1 g/100 g) is estimated as approximately 60% of total fat, consistent with the ghee-dominant fat profile of this dish and USDA FoodData Central ghee data (SR Legacy #04134). 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite traditional preparation are not individually reported in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2024) or USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish. 3. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate based on legume-based halwa preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement exists for Moong Dal Halwa. 4. Portions array in input contained three identical duplicate entries (80 g each); deduplicated to a single 80 g default serving. 5. Food group reclassified from 'Snacks' to 'Desserts' as Moong Dal Halwa is a traditional Indian sweet (mithai) primarily served as a dessert at festive occasions. 6. Vitamin A value of 120 mcg RAE is sourced from ghee and milk/khoya components per ICMR-NIN; interpreted as mcg RAE. 7. Vitamin B12 (0.3 mcg/100 g) is plausible given the milk/khoya content of the dish.
